module_map.responsibility: HTTP request/response handling, cookie parsing, multipart parsing, HttpRequest/HttpResponse classes
Request processing pipeline - loads middleware, dispatches to URL resolver, executes views, handles exceptions. Includes both sync (BaseHandler) and async (ASGI) handlers.
URL routing and resolution - maps URLs to views, supports named URLs, namespace handling
View layer - generic views, decorators, CSRF handling, debugging, i18n
ORM layer - models, queries, migrations, transactions, database backends
Request/response processing hooks - common middleware, security, caching, CSRF, gzip
Template engine - template loading, rendering, context processors, template tags
Form handling - form validation, widgets, fields, media handling
Configuration - global settings, Django settings object, app configuration
Utility functions - datetime, encoding, functional helpers, caching, logging
Optional extensions - auth, admin, sessions, messages, staticfiles, GIS, contenttypes
Application registry - tracks installed apps, provides app configuration

pipeline_trace.description: Raw HTTP request is parsed into HttpRequest object. Handles query strings, POST data, cookies, headers. MultiPartParser handles file uploads.
Each middleware's process_request() is called in order. CommonMiddleware handles redirects, SecurityMiddleware adds headers, CsrfViewMiddleware checks CSRF tokens, SessionMiddleware loads sessions.
URLResolver matches the request path against URL patterns. Supports regex and path converters. Returns ResolverMatch containing view callable, args, kwargs, and URL name.
BaseHandler.resolve_request() calls the resolved view. For class-based views, dispatch() routes to appropriate method (get, post, etc.). View is wrapped with middleware processing.
View may interact with ORM - QuerySet operations, Model.save(), Manager methods. Database backends abstract SQL generation. Signals fire on model operations (pre_save, post_save, etc.).
Template engine renders context data with template files. Supports template inheritance, include, custom tags/filters. Context processors inject request-aware data.
Each middleware's process_response() is called in reverse order. Middleware can modify response, set headers, handle caching, gzip compression.

HttpResponse is sent to client. Handles cookies, session handling, content type, caching headers. For WSGI: WSGIHandler writes to environ['wsgi.output']. For ASGI: ASGIHandler sends via ASGI protocol.

cross_module_hubs.reason: Central request processing hub that orchestrates middleware chain, URL resolution, view dispatch, and exception handling. Both WSGI and ASGI entry points go through BaseHandler. Loads and executes middleware stack.

HttpRequest object flows through the entire pipeline - from input parsing through middleware, views, ORM, templates, and response. All modules interact with request data (headers, cookies, session, user).
ORM is the data layer hub - models are used by forms, views, admin, migrations, signals. QuerySet is the primary interface for data access. All database operations flow through this module.

change_proposal.feature_summary: Add async-compatible structured logging middleware that captures per-request metadata (view name, ORM query count, response time) and emits structured JSON log entries.
files_to_modify: django/core/handlers/base.py (add signals for request_started, request_finished for query counting)
new_types: StructuredLoggingMiddleware: Async-compatible middleware class implementing process_request/process_response and __call__ for ASGI
RequestLogContext: Context manager or dataclass to track request metrics (start_time, view_name, query_count)
log_request: Function to emit structured JSON log with metadata
change_proposal.pattern_to_follow: Existing middleware pattern from django/middleware/common.py and django/middleware/csrf.py - implement both sync (process_request/process_response) and async (__call__) interfaces. Use django.utils.log for logging integration. Follow django.utils.decorators.sync_and_async_middleware for decorator handling.
change_proposal.integration_point: Add StructuredLoggingMiddleware to MIDDLEWARE list (recommended position: first or near CommonMiddleware). It should wrap the request/response cycle and emit logs after response is processed. Query count tracking requires integration with django.db.utils.ConnectionHandler or django.db.backends.base.base - track queries in connection.queries list.
risks: Performance overhead: Adding logging middleware adds latency to every request. Must use efficient logging (no string formatting if not needed).
Query counting complexity: Requires access to database connection queries which may not be available in all configurations. Must handle both sync and async ORM.
Log volume: Structured logs can be large. Consider sampling for high-traffic endpoints.
Compatibility: Must work with both WSGI and ASGI handlers, and with class-based views.
Security: Ensure no sensitive data (passwords, session keys) is logged. Use caution with request.POST and response.content.
